title: Warmup
concept: Warmup begins training with very small learning rates and ramps upward, which helps fragile early updates stay stable before the model reaches full training speed.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What makes cosine decay different from step decay?

options.text: Cosine decay falls smoothly instead of dropping only in big stages.
Cosine decay removes the need for training data.
Cosine decay works only for one-layer networks.

exercises.explain: Right. Cosine decay is about a smooth fall rather than a staircase.

cards.title: Do not sprint out of the tunnel
cards.body: Athletes warm up before a race because full speed on cold muscles can lead to chaos. Some deep networks behave similarly. The very first updates can be surprisingly fragile, so charging in at full learning rate can cause instability before training even gets settled.

cards.title: Small steps before full speed
cards.body: A warmup schedule starts training with a very small learning rate and increases it over the early steps or epochs. After that ramp-up, training usually switches into the main learning rate plan, such as a fixed rate, step decay, or cosine decay.

cards.title: Merging onto the highway
cards.body: When entering a highway, you do not teleport to top speed in the first meter. You accelerate smoothly so the car stays under control. Warmup gives training that same controlled merge into faster learning.

cards.title: The first few hundred steps are the messiest
cards.body: Imagine a deep model whose loss spikes wildly if you start at the full learning rate on batch one. With warmup, the model takes tiny early steps while activations, gradients, and optimizer statistics settle down. Once the start is stable, the learning rate can rise to its normal working level.

cards.title: Warmup is not permanent slowness
cards.body: A common myth is that warmup means training stays cautious forever. It does not. Warmup only covers the opening stretch. Its job is to prevent early chaos, not to make the whole run tiny and timid.

cards.title: Full blast vs. ramp-in
cards.body: Warmup changes the beginning of training: it trades an abrupt launch for a controlled takeoff.

left.text:
Full learning rate immediately.
Can shock unstable early training.
More risk of loss spikes or divergence.

right.text:
Tiny steps at the start.
Gradual ramp to full speed.
More stable early updates.

options.text: When the very start of training is unstable and full-size updates cause spikes or divergence.
When you want training data to disappear after the first epoch.
When your goal is to keep the learning rate tiny forever.
When the optimizer should stop updating weights completely.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Warmup is about stabilizing the fragile opening steps.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Warmup usually affects only the beginning of training, not the entire run.
exercises.explain: True. Warmup is a short ramp-in, after which the main schedule takes over.

exercises.sentence: Warmup starts with very ___ learning rates before ramping up.
exercises.explain: Correct. The whole point is to begin gently and then build up.

options.text: Begin with tiny updates, then gradually increase to the main learning rate.
Start huge and make the rate even bigger every batch forever.
Keep the learning rate fixed and never change it at any point.

exercises.explain: Right. Warmup is a controlled ramp into normal training speed.

exercises.prompt: A large-batch image model often blows up in the first few hundred steps when trained at the full target learning rate, but after that early phase it behaves normally. What is the best schedule idea to add?

options.text: Use warmup so training begins with tiny updates and ramps up after the unstable opening phase.
Keep the full rate from step one and hope the instability disappears on its own.
Remove all gradients during the first part of training.
Judge success only by the number of epochs, not by the loss.

exercises.explain: Exactly. The problem is concentrated at the beginning, which is what warmup is designed to calm down.
